To: The Selection Committee Nordic Africa Institute African Scholarship Programme Uppsala, Sweden Subject: Letter of Support for Lemjini Masandawa African Scholarship Programme 2027 Dear Members of the Selection Committee, I am pleased to give my strongest recommendation for Lemjini Masandawa, a PhD candidate under my supervision at the Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology (NM-AIST). I have supervised him since his Master’s studies in 2020 and I confidently attest to his dedication, research excellence, and strong commitment to addressing public health challenges in Africa through mathematical modelling. Lemjini is a full-time doctoral student and teaching assistant with no access to international PhD funding. He is fully eligible under category 1 (PhD students based in Africa), and this opportunity is critical for advancing his work. His research on mathematical modelling of malaria and soil-transmitted helminth (STH) coinfection dynamics is highly relevant to sub-Saharan Africa and contributes directly to SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being). It integrates key social and behavioural dimensions, including intervention uptake, aligning well with the Institute’s focus on African social sciences and development. His work aims to generate policy-relevant evidence for integrated, cost-effective disease control strategies. A research stay at the Nordic Africa Institute will strengthen the quality and policy relevance of his research through access to interdisciplinary expertise, specialized resources, and academic networks. It will also support the development of a policy-oriented component of his thesis and enhance his ability to communicate findings to policymakers. Lemjini’s work is timely, feasible, and of clear societal relevance. He has strong potential to become a leading researcher in infectious disease modelling in Africa. I recommend him without reservation for the African Scholarship Programme 2027 and am confident he will both benefit from and contribute significantly to the Institute’s research environment. Please feel free to contact me for further information. This is the letter written by the supervisor for supporting grant application of the following proposal. Please revise the letter as per applicant title of PhD research, what he has done so far and what is he going to do next (i mean the second objective is agent base model because he has finished population model). Make the letter brief and attractive for funder to provide lemjini the research visit. Here is the proposal :\documentclass[11pt]{article} \usepackage[a4paper, margin=1in]{geometry} \usepackage{setspace} \usepackage{amsmath, amssymb} \usepackage{graphicx} \usepackage{booktabs} \usepackage{hyperref} \usepackage{tikz} \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning} \onehalfspacing \title{\textbf{Modeling Gendered Inequalities in Malaria Transmission in Tanzania:}\ \textbf{An Agent-Based and Policy-Oriented Approach}} \author{Masandawa Mollel} \date{} \begin{document} \maketitle \section{Introduction} Malaria remains a leading public health challenge in Tanzania, accounting for approximately 4% of global malaria deaths, with over 90% of the population living in areas of active transmission \cite{who2023, tdhs2022}. The burden is substantial, with millions of clinical cases reported annually and considerable geographical heterogeneity, ranging from low prevalence in highland regions to over 20% in lake zones. Importantly, malaria burden is not evenly distributed across populations. Epidemiological evidence consistently shows that adult women experience higher infection rates than men. These disparities are not primarily biological but are strongly shaped by gendered social and behavioural factors. In rural Tanzania, gender roles structure daily routines in ways that influence exposure to mosquito bites. Women commonly engage in domestic activities such as cooking and water collection during early evening and early morning hours, which coincide with peak mosquito biting periods \cite{finda2019}. In contrast, men are more likely to engage in outdoor activities later in the evening or at night. These differences in time-use patterns lead to systematic differences in exposure risk. Gender inequalities also extend to healthcare access. Women often face structural and sociocultural barriers that delay treatment-seeking, including limited financial autonomy and reduced decision-making power. As a result, infections among women may persist longer, increasing both disease severity and transmission potential \cite{hildon2020}. Despite these well-documented dynamics, most malaria transmission models rely on homogeneous assumptions that mask behavioural and social heterogeneity \cite{smith2019}. Classical models can describe gender disparities but do not explicitly represent the mechanisms that generate them, limiting their usefulness for designing targeted interventions. This study addresses this gap by developing a gender-sensitive agent-based model (ABM) of malaria transmission in rural Tanzania. By explicitly modelling individual behaviours, movement patterns, and healthcare access, the study aims to quantify how gendered social structures shape malaria dynamics and to evaluate interventions that reduce inequities. This work contributes to broader efforts toward achieving equitable health outcomes in line with Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 3 and SDG 5). \vspace{0.3cm} \noindent This research forms part of a broader PhD project on population-based and agent-based modelling of malaria transmission dynamics. While earlier work has focused on population-level models, the present study advances this research by developing an agent-based framework under the supervision of Dr. Miralce at LUT, whose work has significantly contributed to agent-based and hybrid epidemiological modelling \cite{amadi2022}. \section{Research Aims and Scope} \subsection{Overall Aim} To develop and analyse a gender-sensitive agent-based model of malaria transmission that captures behavioural and structural determinants of inequality in rural Tanzania. \subsection{Specific Objectives} \begin{itemize} \item To model gender-specific exposure patterns based on daily activities and spatial movement. \item To incorporate gendered differences in healthcare access and treatment-seeking behaviour. \item To quantify the contribution of behavioural and structural factors to gender disparities in malaria burden. \item To evaluate gender-responsive intervention scenarios using simulation. \end{itemize} \subsection{Research Questions} \begin{itemize} \item To what extent do gendered daily activities explain differences in malaria exposure? \item How does unequal access to healthcare influence infection duration and transmission? \item Which interventions are most effective in reducing gender disparities in malaria burden? \end{itemize} \section{Literature Review} Mathematical models have long been used to study malaria transmission, particularly through the Ross--Macdonald framework, which describes interactions between human and mosquito populations. These models have identified key drivers such as mosquito biting rates and recovery rates \cite{smith2019}. However, they typically assume homogeneous populations. Recent studies have attempted to incorporate heterogeneity by stratifying populations by age and sex. For example, Uwineza et al. (2024) demonstrated that adult women contribute disproportionately to malaria transmission. However, such models often assign fixed parameters to gender groups without capturing the underlying behavioural mechanisms. Empirical studies highlight that gender differences in malaria risk are driven by exposure and access. Time-use studies show that women are more exposed during peak mosquito activity periods \cite{finda2019}, while healthcare studies demonstrate delayed treatment among women due to social constraints \cite{hildon2020}. Agent-based models provide a flexible framework to integrate these factors. By simulating individuals with heterogeneous attributes and behaviours, ABMs enable the exploration of how social determinants shape disease dynamics \cite{macal2010}. However, few studies have applied ABMs to explicitly model gender inequalities in malaria, representing a critical gap in the literature. \section{Research Design and Methods} This study adopts an agent-based modelling approach to simulate malaria transmission in a spatially explicit rural Tanzanian setting. The model consists of human and mosquito agents interacting within an environment composed of households, farms, water sources, and communal areas. Human agents are characterised by gender, infection status, behavioural schedules, exposure levels, and healthcare access. Gender determines activity patterns, which in turn influence exposure to mosquito bites. Daily routines are modelled using probabilistic schedules derived from empirical studies. Female agents are more likely to engage in peri-domestic outdoor activities during early evening (cooking) and early morning (water collection), while male agents are more likely to engage in outdoor activities later in the evening or at night. These schedules determine movement across locations with varying mosquito densities. Exposure is modelled dynamically as a function of time spent in different environments:

Eg(t)=∑lβl⋅Tg,l(t),E_g(t) = \sum_{l} \beta_l \cdot T_{g,l}(t),Eg(t)=l∑βl⋅Tg,l(t),

where Tg,l(t) T_{g,l}(t) Tg,l(t) represents time spent in location l l l and βl \beta_l βl represents location-specific biting intensity. Protective behaviours such as insecticide-treated net use are incorporated using gender-specific probabilities. Net usage reduces exposure during sleeping hours. At each time step, mosquito agents move and interact with nearby humans. A biting event occurs probabilistically, and infection is determined by:

Pinfection=1−exp⁡(−Eg(t)).P_{\text{infection}} = 1 - \exp(-E_g(t)).Pinfection=1−exp(−Eg(t)).

Recovery is influenced by healthcare access, modelled as:

Precovery=γ0Ag,P_{\text{recovery}} = \gamma_0 A_g,Precovery=γ0Ag,

where Ag A_g Ag captures gender-specific access to treatment. The simulation is run over a one-year period with hourly time steps. Multiple stochastic simulations are conducted to capture variability. To assess inequality, the model computes gender-specific prevalence (Pf P_f Pf, Pm P_m Pm) and defines a Gender Equity Index:

GEI=PfPm.\text{GEI} = \frac{P_f}{P_m}.GEI=PmPf.

Scenario analysis is conducted to evaluate interventions, including reducing exposure (e.g., protective behaviours during cooking), improving healthcare access for women, and combined strategies. Counterfactual simulations are used to isolate the contribution of behavioural versus structural factors. \section{Implications and Contribution to Knowledge} This study contributes to both methodological and policy-relevant knowledge. Methodologically, it advances malaria modelling by integrating social determinants into an agent-based framework, moving beyond homogeneous assumptions. Substantively, it provides a mechanistic understanding of how gendered behaviours and inequalities drive malaria transmission. By quantifying the contribution of exposure and healthcare access, the study identifies key pathways through which disparities arise. From a policy perspective, the model provides a tool for evaluating gender-responsive interventions and assessing their impact on both overall disease burden and equity. The inclusion of explicit equity metrics ensures that intervention effectiveness is assessed not only in terms of reduction in prevalence but also in terms of fairness and inclusion. \subsection{Conceptual Framework} \begin{center} \begin{tikzpicture}[ node distance=2.8cm, every node/.style={draw, rectangle, rounded corners, align=center, minimum width=3.2cm, minimum height=1.1cm}, arrow/.style={->, thick} ] % Top level \node (gender) {Gender Norms and Roles}; % Second level \node (behavior) [below left=of gender, xshift=-1cm] {Daily Activities\and Mobility Patterns}; \node (access) [below right=of gender, xshift=1cm] {Healthcare Access\and Decision-Making}; % Third level \node (exposure) [below=of behavior] {Exposure Level (Eg E_g Eg)}; \node (treatment) [below=of access] {Treatment Access (Ag A_g Ag)}; % Fourth level \node (interaction) [below=2cm of gender] {Human--Vector Interaction}; % Fifth level \node (infection) [below=of interaction] {Malaria Transmission}; % Sixth level \node (outcomes) [below=of infection] {Health Outcomes}; % Final level \node (equity) [below=of outcomes] {Gender Equity Index (GEI)}; % Arrows \draw[arrow] (gender) -- (behavior); \draw[arrow] (gender) -- (access); \draw[arrow] (behavior) -- (exposure); \draw[arrow] (access) -- (treatment); \draw[arrow] (exposure) -- (interaction); \draw[arrow] (interaction) -- (infection); \draw[arrow] (treatment) -- (infection); \draw[arrow] (infection) -- (outcomes); \draw[arrow] (outcomes) -- (equity); % Feedback loop \draw[arrow, dashed] (equity.west) .. controls +(-3,0) and +(-3,0) .. (gender.west); \end{tikzpicture} \end{center} \noindent This framework illustrates how gender norms shape behavioural patterns and healthcare access, which determine exposure to mosquito bites (Eg E_g Eg) and treatment access (Ag A_g Ag). These pathways influence human--vector interactions and malaria transmission dynamics. The resulting health outcomes are evaluated using both epidemiological measures and a Gender Equity Index (GEI), while policy interventions can modify these pathways to reduce inequalities. \begin{table}[h!] \centering \caption{Tentative Three-Month Work Plan} \begin{tabular}{p{2.5cm} p{10cm}} \toprule \textbf{Month} & \textbf{Key Activities} \ \midrule \textbf{Month 1} & Literature review; develop conceptual framework; identify parameters and data sources; design agent-based model structure. \ \textbf{Month 2} & Implement model in NetLogo/Python; integrate gender-specific behaviours (Eg E_g Eg, Ag A_g Ag); test and validate model components. \ \textbf{Month 3} & Run simulations; conduct scenario analysis; compute GEI; analyse results and prepare report. \ \bottomrule \end{tabular} \end{table} \section{References} \begin{thebibliography}{99} \bibitem{who2023} World Health Organization (2023). \textit{World Malaria Report}. \bibitem{tdhs2022} Tanzania Ministry of Health (2022). \textit{TDHS-MIS Report}. \bibitem{smith2019} Smith, D. et al. (2019). Malaria modelling. \textit{Trends in Parasitology}. \bibitem{macal2010} Macal, C., & North, M. (2010). Agent-based modelling. \bibitem{finda2019} Finda, M.F. et al. (2019). Human behaviour and malaria exposure. \bibitem{hildon2020} Hildon, Z. et al. (2020). Gendered decision-making. \bibitem{amadi2022} Amadi, M. (2022). Hybrid modelling methods. \end{thebibliography} \end{document}. Revise the supporting letter to fit the proposal

To: The Selection Committee Nordic Africa Institute African Scholarship Programme Uppsala, Sweden

Subject: Letter of Support for Lemjini Masandawa – African Scholarship Programme 2027

Dear Members of the Selection Committee,

I am pleased to provide my strongest recommendation for Lemjini Masandawa, a PhD candidate under my supervision at the Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology (NM-AIST). I have supervised him since the beginning of his Master’s studies in 2020 and can attest without reservation to his exceptional dedication, analytical rigour, and passion for using mathematical modelling to address pressing public health challenges in Africa.

Lemjini is a full-time doctoral student and teaching assistant with no access to international funding. He is fully eligible under Category 1 (PhD students based in Africa), and a research stay at the Nordic Africa Institute would be transformative for his work.

His PhD research examines malaria transmission dynamics in Tanzania through both population-based and agent-based modelling. He has successfully completed the population-level component and is now advancing to the second major objective: developing a gender-sensitive agent-based model (ABM) titled “Modeling Gendered Inequalities in Malaria Transmission in Tanzania: An Agent-Based and Policy-Oriented Approach.” This innovative work explicitly incorporates gendered social and behavioural realities—daily activity patterns, mobility, and unequal healthcare access—that drive higher malaria burden among adult women in rural Tanzania. By moving beyond the homogeneous assumptions of classical models, Lemjini’s ABM will quantify the mechanisms behind these disparities and evaluate targeted, gender-responsive interventions. The study directly supports SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being) and SDG 5 (Gender Equality) and is firmly rooted in the lived realities of rural Tanzanian communities.

A three-month research visit at the Nordic Africa Institute would be the ideal platform to elevate this work. It would give Lemjini access to the Institute’s interdisciplinary expertise in African social sciences, development studies, and policy analysis, enabling him to strengthen the policy-oriented component of his thesis, refine his ability to translate complex modelling outputs into actionable recommendations for national and regional policymakers, and build lasting academic networks across the Nordic and African research communities. His project is timely, methodologically innovative, and of clear societal relevance. He has already shown the capacity to produce high-quality, policy-relevant research and is poised to become one of Africa’s next generation of leading infectious-disease modellers.

I recommend Lemjini Masandawa in the strongest possible terms for the African Scholarship Programme 2027. He will both benefit enormously from and make a meaningful contribution to the Institute’s vibrant research environment.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any additional information.

Yours sincerely,

[Your Name] [Your Position] [Department & Institution] [Contact Email and Telephone]

To: The Selection Committee Nordic Africa Institute African Scholarship Programme Uppsala, Sweden

Subject: Letter of Support for Lemjini Masandawa – African Scholarship Programme 2027

Dear Members of the Selection Committee,

I am pleased to give my strongest recommendation for Lemjini Masandawa, a PhD candidate under my supervision at the Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology (NM-AIST). I have supervised him since his Master’s studies in 2020 and can confidently attest to his outstanding dedication, analytical rigour, and commitment to addressing public health challenges in Africa through advanced mathematical modelling.

Lemjini is a full-time doctoral student and teaching assistant with no access to international PhD funding. He is fully eligible under Category 1 (PhD students based in Africa). His doctoral research focuses on modelling malaria transmission dynamics in Tanzania, combining population-based and agent-based approaches. He has successfully completed the population-level modelling component and is now advancing to the second major objective: developing a gender-sensitive agent-based model (ABM) titled “Modeling Gendered Inequalities in Malaria Transmission in Tanzania: An Agent-Based and Policy-Oriented Approach.”

This timely and innovative work explicitly incorporates gendered social and behavioural factors—daily activity patterns, mobility, and unequal healthcare access—that drive disproportionately higher malaria burden among adult women in rural Tanzania. By moving beyond homogeneous assumptions of classical models, the study quantifies the mechanisms behind these disparities and evaluates targeted, gender-responsive interventions. The research directly contributes to SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being) and SDG 5 (Gender Equality) and is deeply rooted in the realities of rural Tanzanian communities.

A research stay at the Nordic Africa Institute would significantly strengthen the quality and policy relevance of his thesis. It would provide access to interdisciplinary expertise in African social sciences and development studies, enable him to enhance the policy-oriented component of his work, and improve his capacity to communicate modelling results effectively to policymakers. The visit would also allow Lemjini to build valuable academic networks while contributing fresh perspectives to the Institute’s research environment.

Lemjini has demonstrated strong potential to become a leading researcher in infectious disease modelling in Africa. I recommend him without reservation for the African Scholarship Programme 2027 and am confident he will both benefit from and enrich the Institute’s vibrant academic community.

Please feel free to contact me for any further information.

Yours sincerely,

[Your Full Name] [Your Position / Title] [Department] Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology (NM-AIST) [Email Address] | [Telephone Number]

Now I am revision of my manuscript where I got comments which make like to restructure all three objectives. NOw my concern since It is online and I have redesign the objectives of research I am allowed to change the whole protocol online. Is it ethical or what can I do

 
 
 
 
 
Thought for 26s

Yes, it is completely ethical — and in fact, the recommended and standard practice — to update your PROSPERO protocol when you make substantive changes to the objectives or methods.

PROSPERO (the international prospective register of systematic reviews) was specifically designed to support transparency. It explicitly allows and encourages amendments to published records. The original version of your protocol remains permanently accessible in the public audit trail/version history, so there is full transparency about what was planned initially versus what was actually done. This is exactly why PROSPERO exists — to prevent hidden changes while still allowing legitimate updates.

What you should do (step-by-step)

  1. Log into your PROSPERO account → Go to “My PROSPERO records” → Open your registered record.
  2. Choose “Major revision” (not “Minor amendment”). Restructuring all three objectives counts as a change to the review methods/objectives, so the system will give you full access to edit the entire record.
  3. Update the relevant sections (especially the objectives, eligibility criteria/PICOS if affected, and any related methods).
  4. In the “Revision notes” field (this is very important), write a clear, brief explanation such as: “Major amendment: The three review objectives have been restructured in response to peer-reviewer comments received during manuscript revision. This change improves clarity, methodological alignment, and relevance of the review. The original protocol (Version X) remains publicly available for reference. All changes are documented in this updated version.”
  5. Submit the revised record for publication. PROSPERO will publish the new version (with a new version number/date) while keeping the full history visible. The process is quick and straightforward.

How to handle this in your manuscript revision

In the Methods section (or in the “Protocol and registration” subsection), add a transparent statement. Example:

“The review protocol was prospectively registered in PROSPERO (CRD420XXXXXX; original version). Following peer review, a major amendment was made to restructure the three objectives for improved clarity and alignment with the available evidence (see updated PROSPERO record, Version Y, for full details and revision notes). All amendments were made prior to final data synthesis and are reported transparently in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines.”

This approach satisfies journal editors, reviewers, and best-practice standards (PRISMA, Cochrane, etc.). Reviewer-driven changes like yours are common and viewed positively when documented properly.

Bottom line

  • You are not only allowed to update the protocol — you should update it.
  • It strengthens the credibility of your manuscript rather than weakening it.
  • Not updating and then presenting changed objectives in the paper would actually be less transparent.

If you want, you can share the exact wording of your original vs. new objectives here and I can help you draft the precise revision notes or the paragraph for your manuscript. You’ve done the right thing by registering the protocol initially — now just keep the transparency going with this update. This is normal academic practice. Good luck with the revision!

Mathematical transmission models play a central role in informing control strategies and World Health Organization (WHO) targets. However, no systematic synthesis has examined how model structure, intervention design, and predicted timelines jointly influence progress toward WHO 2011--2020 and 2021--2030 goals. \textbf{Objective} To classify STH transmission models, decompose interventions into key components, and assess alignment of model outputs with WHO targets, including projected timelines. \textbf{Methods} We searched Web of Science, Scopus, and Embase for English-language studies published between January 2015 and December 2024. Five reviewers independently screened and extracted data. Models were classified by transmission unit, framework, and population structure. Interventions were decomposed into strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. Methodological quality was appraised using assessment of modeling studies tool. \textbf{Principal findings} 43 studies were included in the review. Parasite-based models predominated (70%), compared to host-based models (30%). Deterministic approaches were the most common (53%), followed by stochastic models (40%). Preventive chemotherapy alone was most frequently modeled (81%), while integrated strategies appeared in 19%. Alignment with WHO targets was inconsistent: 60% addressed 2011--2020 goals, 40% addressed 2021--2030 targets, and only 35% reported explicit timelines. Predicted timelines ranged from 2 to 15 years. Methodological gaps included limited validation (14%) \textbf{Conclusions} STH models show substantial heterogeneity and limited alignment with WHO targets. Sparse time-bound predictions and weak validation reduce policy relevance. Future models should standardize reporting, strengthen validation, and explicitly assess timelines for achieving WHO 2030 target \noindent{\bf Keywords:} mathematical modeling, soil-transmitted helminths, intervention strategies, WHO targets, systematic review \section*{Author summary} Soil-transmitted helminthiases (STH) are parasitic worm infections affecting over 1.5 billion people, predominantly in low-resource settings. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set targets to reduce disease burden and interrupt transmission. Mathematical models are increasingly used to guide these efforts, but it remains unclear how well existing models support progress toward these goals. We reviewed 43 modelling studies published between 2015 and 2024 to examine how STH transmission models are structured, how interventions are represented, and how model predictions align with WHO targets. Most studies used parasite-based models, which capture key biological processes such as worm reproduction, while fewer used host-based approaches. Preventive chemotherapy was the most commonly modelled intervention, and strategies targeting entire communities generally showed greater reductions in transmission than those focusing only on children. However, results varied depending on transmission setting and modelling assumptions. We found that evidence linking models to WHO targets is limited and inconsistent. Only a minority of studies provided clear timelines for achieving control or elimination, and relatively few evaluated recent WHO 2021--2030 targets or integrated intervention strategies. In addition, some important infections, such as \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis}, were rarely included. Overall, current modelling provides useful insights but does not yet offer a clear or consistent basis for assessing progress toward WHO targets. Strengthening alignment with policy goals, improving reporting of timelines, and expanding the scope of models could enhance their value for guiding STH control programmes. \linenumbers \section*{Introduction} Soil-transmitted helminthiasis (STH) is among the diseases classified by the World Health Organization (WHO) as neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), reflecting its disproportionate burden in low-resource settings and limited research investment \cite{neto2023we}. STH comprises infections caused by four major nematode species: \textit{Ascaris lumbricoides}, \textit{Trichuris trichiura}, and the hookworms \textit{Necator americanus} and \textit{Ancylostoma duodenale}, with \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis} increasingly recognized as an additional contributor to disease burden \cite{neto2023we,ng2024anthelmintic,mindu2025comparative}. Globally, more than 1.5 billion people are infected across approximately 166 endemic countries, with an estimated 4.5 billion individuals at risk \cite{okoyo2021modeling,world2017guideline,world2024guideline}. The associated burden, measured in disability-adjusted life years (DALYs), ranges from 5 to 39 million, reflecting substantial impacts on population health \cite{keiser2008efficacy}. STH disproportionately affects impoverished and marginalized populations, particularly in regions with inadequate access to safe water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH), including sub-Saharan Africa, parts of Asia, and Latin America \cite{moser2017efficacy}. Chronic and heavy infections are associated with anemia, malnutrition, impaired physical and cognitive development in children, and reduced productivity in adults, thereby reinforcing cycles of poverty and health inequity \cite{okoyo2021statistical,le2024effectiveness}. Global control efforts have been guided by successive WHO roadmaps. The 2011–2020 roadmap focused primarily on morbidity control through preventive chemotherapy (PC), particularly targeting school-age children (SAC), with subsequent expansion to other at-risk groups such as pre-school children (PSAC) and women of reproductive age (WRA). Despite substantial progress, persistent transmission and rapid reinfection in endemic settings have highlighted limitations of PC only strategies. The current 2021–2030 roadmap therefore emphasizes interruption of transmission and elimination as a public health problem, advocating integrated approaches that combine PC with non-pharmaceutical programmes such as water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH), education campaign and other behavioral interventions \cite{okoyo2023optimal}. Preventive chemotherapy (PC) remains the cornerstone of STH control, involving periodic administration of anthelmintic drugs such as albendazole or mebendazole to at‑risk populations \cite{okoyo2021modeling,vegvari2019human}. In settings where single‑dose albendazole or mebendazole may be insufficient, researchers have proposed dual or triple‑drug therapy the latter defined as the combination of three anthelmintic drugs, for example albendazole, ivermectin, and pyrantel pamoate \cite{world2024guideline,world2017guideline}. Mathematical models have been widely used to understand infectious disease dynamics \cite{masandawa2021mathematical,masandawa2022modeling,paul2022mathematical,paul2023mathematical}. In the context of soil-transmitted helminths (STH), they have become essential tools for analyzing transmission patterns and evaluating intervention strategies. These models support policy decisions by simulating alternative scenarios, estimating timelines for control or elimination, and identifying optimal intervention approaches under varying epidemiological conditions \cite{lambura2020mathematical,oguntolu2024mathematical,chazuka2024strategic}. \subsection*{Rationale of the study} Given the increasing reliance on mathematical models to inform STH control and elimination strategies, it is important to critically assess how this body of evidence has evolved and the extent to which it provides consistent and policy-relevant insights. However, despite the growing number of STH modeling studies, there is currently no systematic review that comprehensively synthesizes this literature. Existing reviews have largely focused on broader aspects of neglected tropical disease (NTD) control \cite{turner2015cost,hollingsworth2015quantitative,montresor2020global,campbell2016complexities}, without specifically identifying, appraising, and synthesizing STH transmission models. As a result, it remains unclear whether the expanding modeling evidence coherently guides progress toward the achievement of World Health Organization (WHO) targets and timelines. A systematic review is therefore needed to address this gap. Using a pre-defined protocol aligned with PRISMA guidelines, a review can provide a more reliable evidence base than findings from individual studies. To address this gaps, in this study, we aimed to (i) systematically classifying models based on their biological and structural features (ii) To decompose STH control interventions represented in mathematical models into a set of orthogonal components and use these to construct a systematic classification framework, and (iii) synthesizing model predictions in relation to WHO-defined targets and timelines. Through this approach, the review develops a comprehensive taxonomy for characterizing STH transmission models and intervention configurations reported in the literature. By aligning these findings with WHO targets, the study provides a structured evidence base to support decision-making on the role of mathematical modeling in achieving the 2030 STH goals.  The study is guided by the following three interconnected research questions: \renewcommand{\theenumi}{\roman{enumi}}% \begin{enumerate}      \item \textbf{Model taxonomy:} What classes of STH transmission models emerge from the literature when categorized by biological assumptions, mathematical formulation, and demographic structure? \item \textbf{intervention taxonomy:} What are the key orthogonal components by which STH control interventions represented in mathematical models can be systematically classified? \item \textbf{Predictive insights for WHO targets:} How do outputs from existing STH transmission models map onto WHO targets (2011--2020 and 2021--2030) and timelines? \end{enumerate} \section*{Materials and methods} This study followed the standard procedure for conducting a systematic review in accordance with the 2020 statement of the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) \cite{page2021prisma,page2021updating}. The use of PRISMA guidelines is intended to minimize bias throughout the systematic review process and enhance the validity of findings related to mathematical modeling of helminthiasis control strategies. A protocol was developed for this investigation and submitted to the PROSPERO international prospective register of systematic reviews (registration ID: PROSPERO 2025 CRD420251090375). The completed PRISMA 2020 checklist and abstract are provided as supporting information (see \nameref{S1 File}). \subsection*{Information sources and search terms} A comprehensive literature search was conducted for peer‑reviewed articles published between January 2015 and December 2024 in three major bibliographic databases: Web of Science, Embase, and Scopus. These databases offer structured search filters (document type, subject area, publication type, language etc.) that helped optimize the retrieval process. Additional literature was obtained by hand‑searching the reference lists of included studies and relevant systematic reviews. The search strategy was developed around three core conceptual domains: model term, intervention term, and disease term. Boolean operators were used to combine these domains, resulting in a standardized search string of the form:
("mathematical model*") AND ("soil-transmitted helminth*") AND ("control strateg*")\text{("mathematical model*") AND ("soil-transmitted helminth*") AND ("control strateg*")}("mathematical model*") AND ("soil-transmitted helminth*") AND ("control strateg*")

where the asterisk (*) serves as a truncation or wildcard operator to capture word variations. Alternative terms, derivatives, and synonyms for each primary keyword were also employed to enhance retrieval. The complete search strings for each database are provided posted online for transparent. \subsection*{Eligibility criteria} The search was limited to papers published between 2015 and 2024 to focus on studies that used mathematical models to inform STH morbidity control or transmission interruption. This ten‑year window ensures relevance to ongoing global health efforts, particularly the WHO roadmaps for 2011–2020 and 2021–2030, while balancing recency, methodological advancement, and manageable review scope. The inclusion criteria were: \begin{enumerate} \item[a] Any mathematical model of STH transmission dynamics, including differential equation models (ordinary, partial, or delay), difference equation models, individual‑based models, stochastic process models, and hybrid models combining multiple approaches. \item[b] Models evaluating one or more STH control strategies. \item[c] Model predictions of infection prevalence, intensity, or elimination thresholds as outcomes. \item[d] Peer‑reviewed journal articles published in English between January 2015 and December 2024. \end{enumerate} The exclusion criteria were: \begin{enumerate} \item[a] Non‑dynamic models: purely statistical models, regression analyses, or machine learning without transmission dynamics. \item[b] Cost‑effectiveness only: models focusing exclusively on cost‑effectiveness without transmission dynamics. \item[c] Diagnostic‑focused: models addressing only diagnostic test performance without evaluating STH transmission dynamics or control interventions. \item[d] Non‑STH helminth models: Models of other helminths without STH components. \item[e]Systematic reviews that do not evaluate STH control interventions (for example reviews that lack an assessment of intervention strategies such as preventive chemotherapy, WASH, or integrated control approaches). \item[f] Grey literature (dissertations, reports, posters, conference presentations/proceedings, abstracts, letters, books, book sections). \item[g] Within‑host and pharmacological models without population‑level transmission dynamics. \end{enumerate} \subsection*{Study selection and consistency check} A single investigator (LM) imported all articles retrieved from the three databases into EndNote for duplicate removal and reference management, then transferred them to the Systematic Review Accelerator tool for additional duplicate checking. The database search yielded 597 articles; reference list searches identified 14 additional records, resulting in 611 total records (see \nameref{S3 File}). After removing 84 duplicates, 527 unique articles proceeded to screening. Screening was conducted in two rounds by five reviewers (LM, MA, SM, IM, SK). In the first round, three reviewers (LM, MA, SM) independently screened the titles and abstracts of the 527 articles based on the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Following this stage, 444 articles were excluded and 83 articles advanced to full‑text screening. In the second round, two independent reviewers (IM, SK) assessed the full texts of the 83 articles; 40 were excluded, leaving 43 studies that met the inclusion criteria. Any disagreements during screening were resolved through discussion, with a third reviewer consulted when consensus could not be reached. The study selection process is summarized in the PRISMA flow diagram (Fig.~\ref{Fig1}). \subsubsection*{Data extraction process, coding of framework, and data synthesis} Data extraction was conducted using Microsoft Excel. The 43 included studies were distributed among five authors: three authors extracted data from 9 studies each, and two authors extracted data from 8 studies each (LM: 9, MA: 9, SM: 9, IM: 8, SK: 8). Extraction was performed independently between March 2–25, 2025. A second reviewer (LM) independently verified a random 20% sample of extracted data to ensure accuracy. The full extraction file, including all extracted variables, extractor information can be observed in \nameref{S4 File}. To enable consistent classification across heterogeneous studies, we developed and applied a structured coding framework across three domains: model characteristics, intervention characteristics, and outcomes with WHO target alignment. Each domain was coded using mutually exclusive categories. \paragraph{Model characteristics.} %Models were categorized onto three independent dimensions: transmission unit, mathematical formulation, and population structure. The unit of coding was the model as described in each study. % %The first coding scheme was based on the transmission unit. Any transmission dynamics model that considers the infection status of an individual host and incorporates the depletion of the susceptible population as a negative feedback was classified as host-based. Otherwise a study was classified as parasite-based when the basic unit of transmission was an individual parasite or a mated pair of parasites and the model described the mean of a distribution of worm burdens (either deterministically or stochastically), with negative feedback typically entering as a reduction in female worm fecundity as total worm burden increased. % %Another classification approach was based on mathematical formulation. A study categorized as deterministic if the model averaged system behavior using differential or difference equations and produced a single unique trajectory for any given set of parameters and initial conditions. Also any study was coded as stochastic if the model incorporated random processes affecting transmission, treatment, or demography. Further a study was coded as a deterministic–stochastic extension if the core model remained deterministic but included stochastic elements (for example, noise terms) for sensitivity or comparative analysis. % %A third classification approach considered demographic structure. A model was categorized as homogeneous if it included no demographic stratification, and as age-structured if it explicitly incorporated age groups with differential parameters for exposure, infection risk, or treatment coverage. All dimensions were coded independently. We developed a standardized classification framework for each included model based on three dimensions: transmission unit, mathematical framework, and population structure. The first coding scheme was based on the transmission unit. Any transmission dynamics model that considers the infection status of an individual host and incorporates the depletion of the susceptible population as a negative feedback was classified as host-based. Otherwise a study was classified as parasite-based when the basic unit of transmission was an individual parasite or a mated pair of parasites and the model described the mean of a distribution of worm burdens (either deterministically or stochastically), with negative feedback typically entering as a reduction in female worm fecundity as total worm burden increased. Mathematical framework was classified as deterministic (differential or difference equations), stochastic (individual‑based or random processes) or comparative (if compare deterministic and stochastic output). Population structure was recorded as age‑structured or flat (homogeneous model with not demographic stratification). When a study presented multiple model variants, we classified the primary model as defined by the authors for their main analysis. Studies that employed both a deterministic model and a stochastic model with distinctly different structures, and used them for direct comparison, were classified as comparative frameworks. Two reviewers independently classified each model; disagreements were resolved by consensus, with a 10% random sample double‑extracted to confirm reliability . \paragraph{Intervention characteristics.} Further, we decomposed each modeled intervention into four orthogonal components: strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. Strategy was classified as preventive chemotherapy alone (mass drug administration only) or integrated (any combination with water, sanitation, and hygiene [WASH], behavior change, or vector control). Delivery platform was recorded as school‑based, community‑based, or mixed. Target population was defined as entire community, school‑aged children only, preschool‑aged children only, or other. Treatment regimen captured drug type (albendazole, mebendazole, or combination), frequency (annual, biannual, or other), and reported coverage level (as a percentage or qualitative range). When a study modeled multiple intervention scenarios, we extracted each distinct combination separately for synthesis but reported the primary scenario in summary statistics. If an intervention component was not explicitly described (example delivery platform omitted), we recorded it as not reported. Five reviewers independently extracted these components using a standardized data extraction form. \paragraph{WHO target alignment and timeline.} A standardized extraction framework was used to capture how studies linked model outputs to World Health Organization (WHO) targets. Extracted data included the referenced WHO roadmap (2011–2020 or2021–2030), model outputs used to assess progress (moderate-to-heavy intensity infection (MHI), transmission interruption, elimination), reported timelines (in years), and associated factor for the timeline (treatment intensity, drug efficacy compliance pattern and an intervention strategy). Alignment was assessed using two binary indicators. Studies were classified as aligned with the 2011–2020 targets if they modeled reduction of MHI prevalence to <1 <1 <1% in school-aged children and either were published within that period or explicitly referenced the WHO roadmap. Alignment with the 2021–2030 targets was defined by modeling transmission interruption, elimination, or near-zero prevalence (example <2 <2 <2% across all ages) within the study period (2021–2025). Studies without explicit WHO targets or using non-standard thresholds were classified as not aligned. Timelines were extracted only when explicitly reported. Studies without reported time horizons were classified as having no reported timeline. Data extraction was conducted by five reviewers, with a random subset double-extracted to ensure consistency. A formal meta-analysis was not conducted due to substantial heterogeneity in modeling taxonomy, intervention types, outcomes, and epidemiological assumptions across studies. Instead, we employed a narrative synthesis approach to describe patterns across the coded dimensions, summarize the distribution of model and intervention characteristics, and identify recurring findings related to predictive timelines and WHO target alignment. %The second dimension captured the mathematical formulation, categorizing models as deterministic, stochastic, or deterministic‑stochastic extensions. Deterministic models represent average system behavior using differential or difference equations and yield a single unique trajectory for a given set of parameters and initial conditions. Models were categorized as stochastic if they incorporated random variation in transmission, treatment, or demographic processes. The deterministic‑stochastic extension category was applied to models that were fundamentally deterministic but included stochastic elements (like noise terms) for comparison or sensitivity purposes. % %The third dimension describes demographic structure, distinguishing between homogeneous and age‑structured models. Homogeneous models incorporate no age stratification or other demographic factors, assuming identical infection risks and treatment patterns across all individuals \cite{anderson1991infectious}. In contrast, age‑structured models divide the population into discrete age groups (e.g., pre‑school children, school‑aged children, and adults) and assign age‑specific parameters for exposure, infection risk, or treatment coverage \cite{okoyo2021modeling} %\paragraph{Intervention characteristics.} We decomposed interventions into four orthogonal dimensions: strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. Intervention strategy was classified as preventive chemotherapy (PC) only or integrated (PC combined with non‑pharmacological components such as WASH). Delivery platform refers to the mechanism of intervention delivery and was categorized as school‑based deworming (SBD) targeting children enrolled at schools or community‑based deworming (CBD) delivered at community level with no restriction to school attendance. Target population describes at‑risk groups receiving treatment and was divided into four mutually exclusive categories: school‑aged children (SAC) only, women of reproductive age (WRA) only, community‑wide only, or SAC and adults combined. Treatment regimen captures the pharmacotherapy used and was categorized as single drug (albendazole or mebendazole), dual therapy (e.g., albendazole combined with ivermectin), or triple therapy (Like albendazole + ivermectin + pyrantel pamoate), extracted separately where reported. When studies evaluated multiple intervention configurations, each was coded separately, and counts reflect the number of studies examining each attribute, allowing multiple counts per study. %\paragraph{Intervention characteristics.} %Interventions were coded using a predefined framework comprising four orthogonal dimensions: strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. The unit of coding was the intervention scenario; when a study evaluated multiple scenarios, each was coded separately. %Intervention strategy was classified for every study by determining whether preventive chemotherapy (PC) was employed using albendazole, mebendazole, or ivermectin, or whether integrated PC (example PC supplemented by water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) interventions) was implemented. Both approaches were retained under the single “intervention strategy” category. %Delivery platform was classified according to the explicit mechanism of intervention delivery. A study was coded as school-based deworming (SBD) when the intervention targeted only school-enrolled populations. It was coded as community-based deworming (CBD) when delivery extended beyond schools or explicitly targeted the general population. %Targeted population was classified as school-aged children (SAC) only, women of reproductive age (WRA) only, or community-wide (i.e., all age groups included) when the study explicitly stated these groups. When the study did not explicitly specify the target group, the population was coded as “not specified.” %Treatment regimen was coded strictly according to the number and type of drugs used, as explicitly reported in the paper (single, dual, or triple therapy). % %\paragraph{WHO target alignment and timeline.} %Studies were classified against the WHO 2011–2020 and 2021–2030 roadmaps using two binary indicators: aligned with the 2011–2020 targets, and aligned with the 2021–2030 targets. A study was coded as aligned with the 2011–2020 targets if it explicitly modeled reduction of moderate‑to‑heavy intensity infection (MHI) prevalence to below 1% in school‑aged children, and was either published between 2011 and 2020 or explicitly stated that its targets corresponded to the 2011–2020 WHO roadmap. A study was coded as aligned with the 2021–2030 targets if it explicitly modeled transmission interruption, elimination, or reduction of infection prevalence to near‑zero levels (example, <2% <2\% <2% across all ages), and was published within the study period 2021–2025. Studies that did not explicitly reference WHO targets or used non‑standard thresholds were coded as not aligned with either roadmap. For predictive timelines, we extracted the time horizon explicitly reported by a study for achieving a defined target under a specified intervention scenario. When a study reported a numeric time horizon (example 5 years or 10–15 years), we recorded the reported value; if no such timeline was provided, we coded the outcome as “no timeline” to clearly distinguish studies with and without explicit temporal projections (you can observe this in \nameref{S4 File}) \subsection*{Bibliometric and network analysis} To characterize the research landscape of soil-transmitted helminth (STH) modeling studies, a bibliometric analysis was conducted using VOSviewer (version 1.6.20. Data for analysis were extracted from the titles, abstracts, and author information of the included studies. Co-occurrence analysis of terms was performed to identify major research themes, while co-authorship analysis was used to explore collaboration networks among authors \cite{van2014visualizing}. For the term co-occurrence analysis, a minimum threshold of five occurrences was applied to include terms in the network. VOSviewer’s default clustering algorithm was used to group related terms and authors into clusters based on their co-occurrence patterns. Network and density visualization maps were generated to illustrate the frequency, relationships, and thematic structure of terms, as well as the strength of collaborations among authors. The bibliometric analysis was used to complement the qualitative synthesis by providing insights into dominant research themes, intervention focus areas, and collaboration patterns within the STH modeling literature. \subsection*{Risk of bias and assessment of study quality} Risk of bias was assessed to evaluate the methodological quality of individual studies and its implications for the overall strength of evidence related to the study outcomes. We used the Assessment of Modeling Studies (AMS) tool employed by \cite{fone2003systematic}, which is widely applied in evaluating modeling studies in health and economics, focusing specifically on methodological rigor. The adapted AMS tool comprised twelve criteria: clear aims and objectives (A), intervention comparators (C), outcome measures (O), model structure description (S), parameter specification with sources (P), justification of assumptions (As), data quality and uncertainty/sensitivity analysis (D), model validation (V), results presentation (R), discussion and interpretation (Di), declaration of competing interests (CI), and medoling methodology (M) Each criterion was scored on a three-point scale (0 = absent, 1 = partially present, 2 = fully present), following established approaches \cite{harris2016systematic}. The maximum possible score per study was 24. Total scores were converted to percentages and classified into four categories \cite{ogunlade2023systematic}: low (x≤50% x \leq 50\% x≤50%), medium ($50 < x \leq 65%$), high ($65 < x \leq 80%$), and very high (x>80% x > 80\% x>80%). \subsection*{Handling of Missing Data} During data extraction from the 43 included studies, some variables were incompletely reported, including predictive timelines for achieving WHO targets, code availability, model validation, model calibration, and uncertainty analyses (see \nameref{S4 File}). All data were extracted as reported, and no imputation or estimation of missing values was performed due to the heterogeneity of model structures and outputs. Missing information was explicitly coded as “not reported” and did not constitute grounds for study exclusion. These reporting gaps were documented and incorporated into the narrative synthesis as part of the overall evidence assessment. \section*{Results} \subsection*{Trends in the number of published modeling studies} \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=10cm]{Fig1} \caption{PRISMA schematic diagram showing study selection process. There was no identified published article in 2023 based on the applied search strategy. The year 2024 experienced the highest number of publications (8 studies, 19%). Two years (2021 and 2024) highlighted the peaks in publication (7 and 8 articles respectively), while one year, 2022, experienced a low turn in publication output (only 2 publications). Models were categorized basing on: (i) fundamental transmission unit (host-based versus parasite-based), (ii) mathematical framework (deterministic, stochastic, or comparative (deterministic-stochastic)), and (iii) demographic structure (homogeneous or age-structured). \subsection*{Transmission unit} Across the included studies, two main modeling paradigms emerged, distinguished by the unit of transmission: host-based models and parasite-based models. Among these paradigms, host-based models, which treat the infected individual host as the unit of transmission, were identified in thirteen studies (30%). \cite{lambura2020mathematical, oguntolu2024mathematical, chazuka2024strategic, mustapha2020fractional, omede2024approximate, chong2021modelling, chong2022prevalence, lambura2020modeling, patel2024eco, turner2016cost, turner2015economic, werkman2018testing, davis2018seasonally}. Indicated models under this category employed compartmental structures, such as susceptible–infected–recovered frameworks, to represent transitions between health states in which reductions in susceptible populations was the means for their negative feedback. In contrast, parasite-based models, which consider individual worms or worm pairs as the unit of transmission, were accounted for thirty studies (70%). \cite{truscott2015impact, truscott2016soil, truscott2017identifying, truscott2019heterogeneity, truscott2021forecasting, anderson2015should, anderson2017assessing, coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison, coffeng2018predicted, coffeng2024predicting, coffeng2024cost, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021deworming, vegvari2021impact, farrell2018helminth, farrell2018investigating, werkman2017past, werkman2018defining, clarke2019cluster, giardina2019sampling, malizia2021modelling, hardwick2020breakpoint, hardwick2021stochastic, collyer2024transmission, chong2022prevalence, okoyo2021modeling, okoyo2022sensitivity, maddren2024patterns, turner2016analysis}. These were frameworks that model the mean of a distribution of worm burdens, both deterministically and stochastically where the negative feedback enters as a reduction in female worm fecundity as total burden increases. These models were featured with mechanisms such as density-dependent fecundity and mating probability. \subsection*{Mathematical framework} Models were further classified based on the mathematical formulation used to represent their transmission dynamics. Across the 43 studies included in this review, three main types of frameworks were reported: deterministic, stochastic, and comparative (deterministic-stochastic) framework . Deterministic models were reported in twenty-three studies (53%) \cite{lambura2020modeling, oguntolu2024mathematical, truscott2015impact, turner2015economic, mustapha2020fractional, lambura2020mathematical, chazuka2024strategic, chong2021modelling, davis2018seasonally, kasia2016modelling, okoyo2021modeling, okoyo2022sensitivity, patel2024eco, turner2016analysis, anderson2015should, anderson2017assessing, coffeng2018predicted, werkman2017past, werkman2018testing, turner2016cost, turner2015economic, omede2024approximate, chong2022prevalence}. Identified studies under this taxonomy, employed differential or difference equations to represent the average behaviour of the system, where a single unique trajectory was observed for a given set of parameters and initial condition. The reported deterministic frameworks featured on examining equilibrium behaviour, long-term intervention outcomes, and parameter variation across transmission scenarios. Stochastic models were identified in seventeen studies (40%) \cite{truscott2017identifying, werkman2018defining, farrell2018helminth, farrell2018investigating, clarke2019cluster, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021impact, vegvari2021deworming, malizia2021modelling, hardwick2021stochastic, collyer2024transmission, anderson2017assessing, giardina2019sampling, truscott2021forecasting, coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison, truscott2017identifying}. Studies within this category incorporated random variation in transmission, treatment, and demographic processes, thereby capturing inherent system uncertainty. These frameworks were implemented using approaches such as continuous-time Markov chains, stochastic differential equations, and individual-based simulations. Across the included studies, stochastic formulations were primarily used to represent variability in treatment compliance, worm acquisition, and demographic dynamics, as well as to explore probabilistic outcomes under different intervention scenarios. %Three studies (7%) (see Table \ref{table2}) documented the use of both deterministic and stochastic models \cite{hardwick2020breakpoint, coffeng2017comparison, truscott2016soil}. In these studies, a deterministic framework (typically based on partial differential equations or analytic equilibrium analysis describing population-averaged parasite distributions) was applied alongside a separate individual-based stochastic model for comparison reason. Three studies (as can be observed in table \ref{table2}) employed a comparative modelling approach in which a deterministic model and a stochastic model were developed and applied side-by-side to the same research question \cite{hardwick2020breakpoint, coffeng2017comparison, truscott2016soil}. In these studies, the deterministic component provided a population-averaged description of transmission dynamics (using partial differential equations or analytic equilibrium analysis), while the stochastic component incorporated randomness to account for individual-level variation or uncertainty. \subsection*{Population structure} Across the 43 included studies, population structure was examined to assess whether models accounted for demographic heterogeneity, particularly age stratification, or assumed a homogeneous population. Based on this criterion, models were classified into two broad categories: homogeneous (flat) models and age-structured models. Homogeneous (flat) models, which assume a uniform population without explicit demographic stratification, were identified in fifteen studies (35% \cite{lambura2020mathematical, oguntolu2024mathematical, chazuka2024strategic, omede2024approximate, chong2021modelling, chong2022prevalence}. In contrast, age-structured models , were reported in twenty-eight studies (65%) \cite{truscott2015impact, giardina2019sampling, coffeng2015feasibility, okoyo2021modeling, okoyo2022sensitivity, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021deworming, vegvari2021impact, anderson2017assessing, truscott2019heterogeneity}. Under this category, models divide their population into discrete age groups (for example pre‑school children, school‑aged children, and adults) and assigned age‑specific parameters for exposure, infection risk, or treatment coverage. \begin{table}[!ht] \centering \caption{Summary of model taxonomy across 43 studies. Counts represent number of studies; percentages reflect proportions.} \label{table2} \begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|} \hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{\textbf{TRANSMISSION UNIT}} \ \hline \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Count} & \textbf{Percentage} \ \hline Host-Based & 13 & 30% \ Parasite-Based & 30 & 70% \ \hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{\textbf{MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK}} \ \hline Deterministic & 23 & 53% \ Stochastic & 17 & 40% \ Comparative (DM-SD) & 3 & 7% \ \hline \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{\textbf{POPULATION STRUCTURE}} \ \hline Homogeneous (Flat) & 15 & 35% \ Age-Structured & 28 & 65% \ \hline \end{tabular} \end{table} \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=10cm]{Fig3} \caption{Model Taxonomy Distribution (N = 43 studies).} \label{Fig3} \end{figure} Pie charts (Fig.~\ref{Fig3}) illustrate the distribution of models across the three taxonomic dimensions, including transmission unit, mathematical framework, and population structure \section*{Intervention Decomposition and Classification} Mathematical models of soil-transmitted helminth (STH) transmission evaluated a range of intervention approaches, including preventive chemotherapy (PC), community-wide mass drug administration, and strategies combining pharmaceutical and non-pharmaceutical components such as water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH), health education, and shoe wearing \cite{anderson1991infectious, oguntolu2024mathematical}. Across the 43 studies included in this review, interventions were represented as multi-component constructs and classified according to four dimensions: intervention strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. Each study was categorized based on a combination of these attributes. Across the reviewed studies we observed two main intervention strategy; preventive chemotherapy and integrated strategy (as observed in table \ref{table3}). Preventive chemotherapy (PC) was reported in 35 studies (81%). Integrated strategies combining PC with non-pharmacological components such as WASH, health education, or shoe wearing were documented in 8 studies (19%) \cite{okoyo2023optimal, lambura2020mathematical}. Delivery platform referred to the mechanism through which interventions were administered. Across the identified studies two delivery platforms were found: school‑based and community‑based. Community‑based delivery appeared in 38 studies (88%), and school‑based delivery accounted for 5 studies (12%). Target population described the groups receiving the intervention. In total, four categories of target populations were considered: community-wide, school-only, school-aged children plus adults, and women of reproductive age . Interventions delivered to both school-aged children (5–14 years) and adults were documented in 19 studies (44%). Community-wide target populations accounted for 17 studies (39%). School-only targeting of children aged 5–14 years was observed in 5 studies (12%), while interventions directed at women of reproductive age (15–49 years) were noted in 2 studies (5%) \cite{coffeng2015feasibility,vegvari2021deworming}. Treatment regimen described the pharmacological composition and frequency of drug administration. In total, two treatment approaches were observed: single-drug therapy and dual therapy. Single-drug therapy (monotherapy), involving albendazole or mebendazole, was used in 40 studies (93%), whereas dual therapy was employed in 3 studies (7%) \cite{anderson2017assessing,turner2016analysis,farrell2018investigating}. \begin{table}[!ht] \centering \caption{Distribution of intervention dimensions across 43 modeling studies} \label{table3} \begin{tabular}{|p{3.5cm}|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Dimension} & \textbf{Category} & \textbf{Count} & \textbf{Percentage} \ \hline \multirow{3}{*}{Strategy} & PC & 35 & 81% \ & Integrated & 8 & 19% \ \hline \multirow{3}{*}{Platform} & School & 5 & 12% \ & Community & 38 & 88% \ \hline \multirow{5}{*}{Target Population} & Community-wide & 17 & 39% \ & SAC only & 5 & 12% \ & WRA only & 2 & 5% \ & SAC + Adults & 19 & 44% \ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Regimen} & Single therapy & 40 & 93% \ & Dual therapy & 3 & 7% \ \hline \end{tabular} \begin{flushleft} \textbf{Table notes:} PC = preventive chemotherapy; WASH = water, sanitation, and hygiene; SAC = school-aged children; WRA = women of reproductive age. In the gathered evidence, 15 (35%) provided explicit time horizons for achieving WHO-defined targets, expressed as years or projected calendar years. The remaining 28 studies (65%) described intervention outcomes without specifying a time horizon (see \nameref{S4 File} and Fig.~\ref{Fig6}). Among the studies reporting timelines, 14 provided quantitative information on time to target. Of these, 7 studies specified discrete time points (e.g., 2, 5, or 10 years), while 7 studies presented timelines as ranges (e.g., 2–10, 5–6, 3–7, 2–15, 5–10, and 2–3 years) (see Table~\ref{table1}). For targets aligned with the WHO 2011–2020 roadmap, 5 studies presented discrete time points and 4 studies reported timelines as ranges. For the WHO 2021–2030 targets, 2 studies provided discrete time estimates and 3 studies reported ranges. One study presented projected calendar years (as 2028) without specifying a relative duration. \subsection*{Factors Associated with Time to Target} Among studies reporting timelines, several model features and intervention characteristics were described in relation to time to target. From the studies synthesized, 5 factors appear to affect predicted timeline; treatment intensity, drug efficacy, compliance pattern, and control strategy. Twelve studies described treatment intensity, including frequency of administration and coverage levels. Among these studies reported higher number of PC rounds to quick attainment of the target \cite{okoyo2021modeling, coffeng2024cost, truscott2021forecasting, turner2015economic}. Further, they associated higher coverage to lower prevalence. Further, within these studies, coverage was reported in terms of levels: higher coverage (80–90%) and moderate coverage (60–75%) whereas the higher were associated with early attainment of the timeline. Compliance patterns were described in 3 studies \cite{farrell2018helminth, maddren2024patterns, anderson2017assessing} including scenarios with systematic non-compliance and random compliance. They reported that systematic compliance associated with reaching faster to the goal . Three studies included integrated intervention strategies combining preventive chemotherapy with non-pharmacological components such as water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) \cite{coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2018predicted, okoyo2021modeling}. Among the studies which included integrated strategy reported the shorter time line . In the body of evidence reviewed, 28 studies did not report explicit time horizons. These studies instead focused on methodological or theoretical analyses, employed short simulation periods without projecting time to target, presented probabilities of elimination without time estimates, reported outcomes at fixed time points only, or examined post‑intervention dynamics such as reinfection or rebound patterns. \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=8cm]{Fig5} \caption{Distribution of studies according to WHO targets. 26 studies (60%) aligned with WHO 2011--2020 morbidity control targets, and 17 studies (40%) aligned with WHO 2021--2030 elimination targets.} \label{Fig5} \end{figure} \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=8cm]{Fig6} \caption{Time-bound predictions reported by modeling studies. Only 15 studies (35%) explicitly reported time horizons for achieving WHO targets; 28 studies (65%) did not specify predictive timelines.} \label{Fig6} \end{figure} \section*{Results on quality assessment of included studies} The selected studies exhibited score ranges from 70.8% to 95.8%. Of the 43 included studies, 32 (74.4%) were categorized as very high quality (≥80% \geq 80\% ≥80%), 11 (25.6%) as high quality (60–79%), and none as medium (50–59%) or low quality (<50% <50\% <50%). The quality assessment focused exclusively on methodological rigor across twelve criteria: clear aims and objectives (A), intervention comparators (C), outcome measures (O), model structure (S), parameter specification (P), assumptions justification (As), data quality with sensitivity/uncertainty analysis (D), model validation (V), results presentation (R), results discussion and interpretation (Di), competing interests declared (CI), and modeling methods (M). Domain‑specific scores for each criterion are provided in Table~\ref{table5}. Across the 43 studies, the most frequently satisfied criteria were clear aims (100%), outcome definition (100%), results presentation (100%), and modeling methods (100%). The least satisfied criteria were reproducibility (only 5 studies provided code or data, 14%), model validation (reported in 60% of studies), and assumption justification (reported in 65%). These findings indicate that while most studies demonstrated strong methodological design in terms of modeling methods, persistent gaps in validation, transparency of assumptions, and reproducibility limit the overall policy readiness of the STH modeling literature. \begin{adjustwidth}{-2.25in}{0in} \centering \setlength{\LTcapwidth}{\textwidth} \footnotesize
