Three main clusters are observed: (i) helminth infections and intervention strategies, including preventive chemotherapy, sanitation, and WASH (red cluster); (ii) children as the primary target population for interventions (green cluster); and (iii) study settings, including school-aged children (SAC), clusters, communities, and villages (blue cluster). These clusters reflect the dominant thematic focus of STH modeling studies, particularly on the delivery of interventions within defined population groups. \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=8cm]{Fig8} \caption{Density visualization of frequently co-occurring terms. Warmer colors (yellow) indicate higher frequency and co-occurrence density.} \label{Fig8} \end{figure} The density visualization (Fig. \ref{Fig8}) further highlights mass drug administration (MDA), preventive chemotherapy, and elimination as central and highly connected themes within the literature. School-aged children emerge as the primary risk group and focus of intervention strategies. However, this emphasis may overlook other population groups, such as adults in hookworm-endemic settings, who can sustain transmission and limit the effectiveness of child-targeted interventions. \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=10cm]{Fig9} \caption{Co-authorship network of contributing authors. Node size reflects publication frequency; edge thickness indicates collaboration strength; clusters denote closely collaborating groups.} \label{Fig9} \end{figure} The coauthorship network (Fig. \ref{Fig9}) reveals a small number of highly connected collaboration clusters that dominate STH modeling research. Major clusters are centred around groups led by Anderson, Truscott, and Hollingsworth, alongside collaborating groups including Coffeng, Werkman, Farrell, and Hardwick. Notably, a substantial proportion (68%) of included studies involve authors affiliated with the NTD Modelling Consortium \cite{behrend2020modelling}, a coordinated initiative supported by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. This consortium has played a central role in advancing the field by promoting policy-relevant modelling, methodological standardisation, and principles such as transparency, reproducibility, uncertainty quantification, and stakeholder engagement. This suggests that, while collaboration is well established within global networks, participation from researchers embedded in endemic settings, where transmission dynamics and intervention challenges are directly experienced remains limited. Capacity-building initiatives are increasingly addressing this imbalance. The Africa Modelling Network for Neglected Tropical Diseases (AM\textsuperscript{2}NTD), hosted at CEMA in Kenya, strengthens local expertise through training, mentorship, and south--south collaboration \cite{am2ntd_about}. In addition, recent Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation-supported programmes in countries such as Niger and Ethiopia aim to equip local researchers and policymakers with analytical tools to support NTD control and elimination strategies \cite{bluesquare2025training, guardian2025leaders}. Expanding such initiatives could enhance the contextual relevance, uptake, and sustainability of modelling-informed policy in endemic settings and elimination. \begin{figure}[!ht] \centering \includegraphics[width=10cm]{Fig10} \caption{Distribution of helminth species studied across the included modelling articles. H = hookworm, R = roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), W = whipworm (Trichuris trichiura), and S = Strongyloides stercoralis} \label{Fig10} \end{figure} Fig~\ref{Fig10} presents the distribution of helminth species represented in the included modelling studies. Of the 43 studies reporting species-specific information, 12 studies (29%) modelled hookworm only, 4 (7%) modelled \textit{Ascaris lumbricoides} only, 2 (5%) modelled \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis} only, and 1 (5%) modelled \textit{Trichuris trichiura} only. Mixed species modelling was common: 5 studies (12%) included hookworm and \textit{Ascaris lumbricoides}, 16 (40%) included hookworm, \textit{Ascaris lumbricoides}, and \textit{Trichuris trichiura}, and 1 (2%) included all four species.Overall, 22 studies (54%) modelled more than one helminth species. Overall, the distribution indicates that modelling studies most frequently include hookworm either alone or in combination with other species, with mixed species frameworks representing more than half of the studies, while single-species models for \textit{Ascaris lumbricoides}, \textit{Trichuris trichiura}, and \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis} are less common. \subsection*{Model calibration, validation, sensitivity analysis, and uncertainty analysis} To systematically evaluate methodological rigor across the 43 included studies, we distinguished four conceptually distinct approaches: calibration (parameter estimation), validation (predictive performance testing on independent data), sensitivity analysis (assessment of how input variation affects outputs), and uncertainty analysis (quantification of how variability propagates to predictions). The application of these concepts varied considerably across the reviewed literature. In the following subsections, we summarize how each approach was implemented, the frequency of its use, and the overall patterns observed. Table~\ref{table6} provides a high‑level distribution of these methodological approaches across the included studie \subsubsection*{Model Calibration} Calibration refers to the process of estimating unknown parameters by fitting model outputs to observed data. Of 43 studies, 29 (67%) reported formal calibration. Approaches included Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) in 12 studies \cite{okoyo2022sensitivity, werkman2018testing, okoyo2021modeling, turner2016cost, kasia2016modelling, mustapha2020fractional}, Bayesian methods (Markov Chain Monte Carlo, Approximate Bayesian Computation, or Sequential Monte Carlo) in 14 studies \cite{coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison, collyer2024transmission, davis2018seasonally, truscott2019heterogeneity, truscott2021forecasting, truscott2015impact, truscott2017identifying, truscott2016soil, anderson2017assessing}, and Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS) in 3 studies \cite{mustapha2020fractional, kasia2016modelling, okoyo2021modeling}. The remaining 14 studies (33%) used literature-based parameters or did not specify calibration methods \cite{malizia2021modelling, vegvari2021deworming, coffeng2018predicted, clarke2019cluster, coffeng2024cost, chazuka2024strategic, lambura2020mathematical, lambura2020modeling, oguntolu2024mathematical, omede2024approximate, patel2024eco, chong2021modelling, chong2022prevalence, anderson2015should}. \subsubsection*{Model Validation} Validation tests predictive performance on independent data not used for calibration. Studies were classified as performing external validation when model predictions were compared against data from a different setting or time period, and internal validation when data were split (example training versus testing subsets) or when model outputs were compared against withheld data from the same setting. Only 6 studies (14%) conducted external validation. \subsubsection*{Sensitivity Analysis} Sensitivity analysis examines how variation in input parameters or structural assumptions affects model outputs. Applied in 36 studies (84%), approaches were categorized as local (one-at-a-time variation), global (variance-based methods such as Latin Hypercube Sampling with Partial Rank Correlation Coefficients, eFAST, or Sobol indices), or structural (comparing alternative model formulations). Local sensitivity analysis was employed in 22 studies \cite{chazuka2024strategic, lambura2020mathematical, lambura2020modeling, oguntolu2024mathematical, omede2024approximate, patel2024eco, chong2021modelling, werkman2018testing, turner2016cost, werkman2017past, turner2015economic, anderson2015should, farrell2018investigating, farrell2018helminth, malizia2021modelling, hardwick2020breakpoint, hardwick2021stochastic, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021deworming, vegvari2021impact, coffeng2024cost, coffeng2024predicting}. Global sensitivity analysis was applied in 9 studies \cite{coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison, okoyo2022sensitivity, chazuka2024strategic, truscott2019heterogeneity, davis2018seasonally, anderson2017assessing, coffeng2024cost, okoyo2021modeling}. Structural sensitivity analysis, comparing alternative model formulations, was conducted in 8 studies \cite{coffeng2017comparison, vegvari2021deworming, malizia2021modelling, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021impact, truscott2017identifying, coffeng2018predicted, farrell2018investigating}. \subsubsection*{Uncertainty Analysis} Uncertainty analysis quantifies how parameter, structural, or stochastic variability propagates into model predictions. Twenty-seven studies (63%) reported formal uncertainty quantification. Approaches included Bayesian credible intervals (derived from MCMC or ABC posterior distributions) in 16 studies \cite{coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison, truscott2019heterogeneity, truscott2021forecasting, truscott2015impact, truscott2017identifying, truscott2016soil, davis2018seasonally, anderson2017assessing, collyer2024transmission, hardwick2021stochastic, okoyo2021modeling, vegvari2019human, vegvari2021deworming, vegvari2021impact, malizia2021modelling}. Stochastic simulation intervals (derived from repeated simulations) were reported in 13 studies \cite{chazuka2024strategic, coffeng2024predicting, werkman2018defining, werkman2017past, vegvari2019human, farrell2018investigating, farrell2018helminth, hardwick2020breakpoint, hardwick2021stochastic, malizia2021modelling, truscott2019heterogeneity, turner2015economic, turner2016analysis}. Confidence intervals based on asymptotic or bootstrap approximations were reported in 6 studies \cite{okoyo2022sensitivity, okoyo2021modeling, giardina2019sampling, maddren2024patterns, werkman2018testing, coffeng2024cost}. The remaining 16 studies (37%) lacked formal uncertainty quantification. \begin{table}[h] \centering \caption{Summary of methodological approaches across 43 STH modeling studies} \begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|p{5.5cm}|} \hline \textbf{Component} & \textbf{N} & \textbf{%} & \textbf{Predominant Approach} \ \hline Calibration & 29 & 67% & Bayesian (33%), MLE (28%) \ Validation (any) & 6 & 14% & External (14%) \ Sensitivity analysis & 36 & 84% & Local (51%) \ Uncertainty quantification & 27 & 63% & Bayesian credible intervals (37%) \ \hline \end{tabular} \label{table6} \end{table} \section{Discussion} This systematic review established a structured framework for comparing mathematical models of soil-transmitted helminth (STH) transmission and synthesizing their predictions regarding progress toward World Health Organization (WHO) control and elimination targets. By introducing a three dimensional taxonomy for model classification and a four component framework for intervention decomposition, the review provides a systematic basis for interpreting the heterogeneous modelling landscape. The findings show that predictions of timelines and feasibility of achieving WHO targets are not uniform but depend on the interaction between model structure and intervention configuration. The application of the proposed model taxonomy reveals that the STH modeling literature is dominated by parasite-based frameworks (70%), which explicitly represent worm burden, density-dependent fecundity, and mating probabilities. This predominance likely reflects a consensus that capturing these helminth specific biological processes is essential for accurately modeling transmission dynamics and the potential for elimination, as these mechanisms introduce nonlinearities and threshold effects (for instance the Allee effect) that are not captured in host-based models \cite{hardwick2020breakpoint, anderson2015should}. While host-based models (30%) offer analytical tractability and are valuable for exploring broad intervention scenarios, their representation of endemic equilibrium through susceptible depletion may oversimplify the resilience of STH transmission at low prevalence. The distribution of mathematical frameworks, with deterministic (53%) and stochastic (40%) approaches both widely used, indicates that the choice depends on the research question: deterministic models are suitable for exploring average system behavior and equilibrium, while stochastic models are critical for quantifying extinction probabilities and the variability inherent in elimination programs, particularly in the final stages \cite{werkman2018defining}. The prevalence of age-structured models (65%) underscores the recognition that heterogeneity in exposure and treatment access across age groups is a critical determinant of intervention outcomes \cite{truscott2015impact, giardina2019sampling}. Together, these taxonomic dimensions provide a powerful lens for interpreting why different models may yield divergent predictions. Complementing the model taxonomy, the decomposition of interventions into orthogonal components strategy, delivery platform, target population, and drug regimen reveals clear patterns in the literature and their association with predicted outcomes. This taxonomy reveals that while preventive chemotherapy alone remains the most frequently modeled strategy (81%), integrated approaches combining PC with WASH or other interventions are increasingly represented in recent years (19% of studies, with 5 of 8 published after 2021). This shift aligns with the WHO 2021–2030 roadmap's emphasis on integrated, multi-component strategies for sustainable STH control \cite{world2024guideline}. However, the comparative analysis of delivery platforms provides a crucial insight: community-based deworming (CBD) consistently outperforms school-based deworming (SBD) in achieving transmission interruption \cite{truscott2015impact, anderson2015should}. This pattern arises because CBD directly targets the adult population, which acts as an untreated reservoir that can sustain transmission and lead to rapid rebound after the cessation of child only programs. This finding is robust across multiple studies and highlights a fundamental limitation of SBD strategies for achieving the WHO 2030 elimination goals. Furthermore, the limited but growing body of evidence on dual therapy and integrated strategies (PC+WASH) points to promising pathways for accelerating progress, particularly for species like \textit{Trichuris trichiura} where single-drug efficacy is suboptimal \cite{turner2016analysis, okoyo2022sensitivity}. The synthesis of predictive timelines directly links these taxonomic and intervention frameworks to the review's central policy question. The finding that only 35% of studies reported explicit timelines is a critical observation, representing a significant gap in the policy utility of the modeling literature. Among studies reporting timelines, projected durations ranged from 2 to 15 years, depending on intervention characteristics, coverage levels, baseline endemicity, and the specific target (morbidity control versus transmission interruption). These findings are broadly consistent with earlier modeling syntheses \cite{hollingsworth2015quantitative}, but our analysis extends this work by systematically linking timelines to specific intervention components. The alignment of modeling studies with WHO roadmaps also warrants comment. Sixty percent of studies explicitly or implicitly modeled outcomes consistent with the 2011–2020 morbidity control targets, while 40% aligned with the more ambitious 2021–2030 elimination goals. The increasing proportion of studies targeting elimination in recent years (2021–2024) reflects the evolving global policy landscape Finally, the quality assessment reveals a methodological gap: while calibration and sensitivity analysis are common (65% and 84%, respectively), model validation is performed in only 14% of studies ( external validation). This is a critical weakness. A model can be well calibrated to historical data but fail to predict future trends under new intervention strategies. External validation against independent datasets is essential for building confidence in model projections and should be considered a minimum standard for policy-informing models \cite{coffeng2015feasibility, coffeng2017comparison}. Moving towards more rigorous, validated, and transparent modeling practices is essential for translating model-based insights into actionable policy guidance. This systematic review provides the first explicit, rule‑based taxonomy for classifying STH transmission models and intervention configurations, enabling reproducible evidence synthesis. It quantifies major gaps: only 19% of models integrate WASH, 7% use dual therapy, and 35% report timelines for WHO targets. These findings establish a benchmark for aligning modelling practice with the 2030 elimination goals. \section*{Recommendations} The findings of this review, grounded in a structured taxonomy of models and intervention decomposition, support a set of mathematical evidence-informed recommendations derived from consistent patterns across the included studies. \subsection*{Model development and reporting} Adoption of standardized reporting frameworks, may substantially improve comparability across studies and facilitate synthesis. The absence of consistent reporting currently limits the ability to interpret differences in model predictions. Strengthening model validation and uncertainty analysis is also critical. The limited use of external validation and comprehensive uncertainty quantification reduces confidence in model based predictions. Future studies should incorporate validation against independent datasets where possible and explicitly quantify uncertainty in projected outcomes. The evidence base remains limited for several intervention configurations, including integrated strategies, alternative drug regimens, and broader target populations. Expanding modelling efforts in these areas would provide more comprehensive insights, particularly in relation to achieving the WHO 2030 elimination targets. In addition, the limited representation of endemic-country researchers suggests a need for greater investment in capacity building and context-specific modelling. Increasing participation from endemic regions would enhance the relevance of models to local transmission dynamics and implementation realities. \subsection*{Policy and practice} Mathematical models evidence indicated that community-based deworming strategies achieve greater reductions in transmission and shorter timelines compared to school-based approaches, particularly in settings where untreated adult populations sustain transmission. This suggests that broader population coverage may be necessary for achieving elimination goals. Intervention design should also consider species-specific treatment responses. While single-drug therapy predominates in the literature, evidence suggests that alternative regimens may improve outcomes in settings where drug efficacy varies across species. Integrated strategies combining preventive chemotherapy with improvements in water, sanitation, and hygiene show potential for reducing reinfection and supporting long-term control. However, the current evidence base remains limited, and the effectiveness of such approaches appears to depend on local context and implementation assumptions. Finally, predicted timelines should be interpreted with caution. The limited reporting of time-bound predictions, combined with variability in model structure and assumptions, indicates that timelines are better understood as indicative ranges rather than precise estimates for policy planning. \section{Limitations} This systematic review has several limitations. The search strategy, while comprehensive, was restricted to English language publications indexed in three major databases between 2015 and 2024. Relevant studies published before 2015, in other languages, or in other databases may have been missed; however, the focus on the past decade aligns with WHO roadmap periods and captures contemporary modeling practice. The substantial heterogeneity in modeling frameworks, outcomes, and reporting styles precluded formal meta-analysis. The narrative synthesis approach, though appropriate for such diversity, cannot provide quantitative summary estimates or formally assess publication bias, meaning the patterns described should be interpreted as qualitative insights rather than definitive quantitative summaries. The intervention decomposition framework, while novel, required subjective judgments about study categorization. To minimize bias, explicit coding rules were developed, piloted on a subset of studies, and disagreements were resolved through consensus among multiple reviewers. Nevertheless, alternative classification approaches might yield different patterns. practices. Analysis of predictive timelines was constrained by the small number of studies (15, 35%) that reported explicit time horizons. Among these, reporting formats varied (years, treatment rounds, calendar years), limiting direct comparison. The absence of explicit timelines in the majority of studies restricts the ability to draw robust conclusions about the consistency of predictions across model types or intervention configurations. Geographic and species representation gaps further constrain the generalizability of findings. The included studies disproportionately focused on settings in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia, with limited representation of other endemic regions. Similarly, while hookworm was included in 81% of species-specific studies, other species such as \textit{Trichuris trichiura} and \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis} were less frequently modeled. These gaps limit the applicability of findings to regions or species with different transmission dynamics or intervention histories. Finally, the review period (2015–2024) may not capture the full evolution of modeling approaches that informed earlier WHO roadmaps. However, focusing on recent literature ensures relevance to current policy discussions and reflects methodological advancements made over the past decade Despite these limitations, the findings of this review provide a robust evidence base to inform public health strategies aimed at assessing the attainment of the WHO roadmap for STH control and elimination, particularly through the application of mathematical modeling in endemic regions. \section*{Conclusion} This study investigated how the mathematical models developed over the last decade related to WHO 2011–2020 and 2021–2030 targets, and how different model structures and interventions decomposition perform in this context. Analyzing 43 studies published between 2015 and 2024, we found that parasite-based, deterministic, age-structured models predominate, though substantial methodological diversity exists across the literature. Our intervention decomposition framework reveals that while preventive chemotherapy remains the most frequently modeled strategy, integrated approaches are increasingly represented in recent years. Despite generally high methodological quality, important gaps persist where only 14% of studies performed formal external validation. Predictive timelines for WHO targets vary substantially across studies (2--15 years), reflecting differences in intervention characteristics, coverage levels, baseline endemicity, and modeling assumptions. Only 35% of studies reported explicit time horizons, limiting the evidence base for policy planning. Bibliometric analysis reveals concentrated research networks dominated by high-income country institutions, with limited participation from endemic regions, and uneven species coverage, with \textit{Strongyloides stercoralis} and \textit{Trichuris trichiura} underrepresented relative to their burden. To advance the field, we recommend standardized reporting of model structures, strengthened validation practices, integrated sensitivity and uncertainty analyses, enhanced reproducibility, and expanded representation of understudied interventions, populations, and species. For policy and practice, context-specific modeling, investment in integrated intervention strategies, capacity strengthening in endemic countries, and alignment with programmatic realities are essential priorities. As the global community strives to achieve the WHO 2030 targets for STH elimination, robust, transparent, and locally relevant modeling will be indispensable. By synthesizing current evidence, identifying gaps, and proposing actionable recommendations, this review aims to support both modelers and policymakers in advancing the science and practice of STH control toward a future free of preventable helminth infections. \section*{Supporting information} % Include only the SI item label in the paragraph heading. Abstract

Background Soil-transmitted helminthiasis (STH) remains a major public health burden, affecting over 1.5 billion people globally and perpetuating cycles of poverty and malnutrition in low-resource settings. Mathematical transmission models are widely used to inform control strategies and evaluate progress toward World Health Organization (WHO) 2011–2020 morbidity control and 2021–2030 elimination targets. However, the literature lacks a systematic synthesis of how model structures, intervention representations, and predicted timelines align with these policy goals.

Objective To systematically classify STH transmission models, decompose modeled interventions into key components, and assess the alignment of model outputs with WHO targets, including explicit timelines for control or elimination.

Methods We searched Web of Science, Scopus, and Embase for English-language peer-reviewed studies published between January 2015 and December 2024. Five reviewers independently screened titles/abstracts and full texts, extracted data, and appraised methodological quality using an adapted Assessment of Modeling Studies tool. Models were classified by transmission unit, mathematical framework, and population structure. Interventions were decomposed into strategy, delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. A narrative synthesis was performed due to heterogeneity; bibliometric analysis complemented the review.

Principal findings Forty-three studies were included. Parasite-based models predominated (70%), followed by host-based (30%). Deterministic frameworks were most common (53%), with stochastic (40%) and comparative approaches (7%). Age-structured models accounted for 65%. Preventive chemotherapy alone was modeled in 81% of studies, while integrated strategies (e.g., with WASH) appeared in 19%. Only 35% of studies reported explicit timelines for achieving WHO targets (ranging 2–15 years), and alignment with the 2021–2030 roadmap was inconsistent (40%). Methodological gaps included limited external validation (14%) and reproducibility.

Conclusions STH modeling literature shows substantial heterogeneity and limited policy alignment with WHO targets. Sparse time-bound predictions and weak validation reduce the translational value of current models. Future work should adopt standardized reporting, strengthen validation and uncertainty analysis, and better integrate non-pharmaceutical interventions to support sustainable progress toward 2030 elimination goals.

Keywords: mathematical modeling, soil-transmitted helminths, preventive chemotherapy, WHO targets, systematic review, agent-based modeling, intervention strategies

Abstract

Background Soil-transmitted helminthiasis (STH) affects over 1.5 billion people worldwide and continues to impose a significant public health burden in low-resource settings. Mathematical transmission models are increasingly used to simulate STH dynamics and evaluate control strategies aimed at achieving World Health Organization (WHO) morbidity control and transmission interruption targets. However, the rapidly growing body of modelling literature has not been systematically synthesised, leaving uncertainty about how different model structures and intervention representations align with WHO 2011–2020 and 2021–2030 goals.

Objective This systematic review aims to classify STH transmission models, decompose modelled interventions into core components, and assess the alignment of model predictions with WHO targets, including reported timelines for control or elimination.

Methods We will search Web of Science, Scopus, and Embase for peer-reviewed English-language articles published between 1 January 2015 and 31 December 2024. Additional records will be identified through hand-searching of reference lists. Two or more independent reviewers will screen titles and abstracts, followed by full-text assessment against predefined eligibility criteria. Data extraction will be performed using a standardised form.

Mathematical models will be classified according to three dimensions: transmission unit (host-based vs parasite-based), mathematical framework (deterministic, stochastic, or comparative), and population structure (homogeneous vs age-structured). Interventions will be decomposed into four orthogonal components: strategy (preventive chemotherapy alone or integrated), delivery platform, target population, and treatment regimen. Alignment with WHO targets and explicit timelines will be extracted and synthesised narratively. Methodological quality will be appraised using an adapted Assessment of Modeling Studies tool. Bibliometric analysis will complement the synthesis by mapping research themes and collaboration networks.

Expected outcomes This review will provide a structured taxonomy of STH transmission models and intervention configurations. It will identify strengths, gaps, and inconsistencies in how current models support progress toward WHO 2030 targets. Findings are expected to guide future model development, improve reporting standards, and strengthen the policy relevance of mathematical modelling for STH control and elimination in endemic settings.

Keywords: soil-transmitted helminths, mathematical modelling, systematic review protocol, WHO targets, preventive chemotherapy, intervention strategies
